# Twitch-Stats Monorepo: Architecture & Data-Flow Report

## Overall architecture / how pieces communicate

The system is an **event-driven pipeline glued together by Apache Kafka** (kafkajs), with **PostgreSQL** as the system-of-record and **Redis** as a dedup cache. There is no direct service-to-service calling — everything flows through Kafka topics. Deployed on Kubernetes (kustomize manifests under `deploy/`).

Kafka topics (defaults defined in `libraries/config/src/index.ts:69-73`):
- `twitch-stats-streams` — raw stream batches (`streamsTopic`)
- `twitch-stats-streams-id` — user IDs needing a targeted re-poll (`streamsIdTopic`)
- `twitch-stats-exported-stream` — export notifications (`exportTopic`, largely unused)

Pipeline:
```
[streams CronJob] --helix /streams--> Kafka(streams) --+--> [streams-process] --> Postgres
                                                        +--> [missing] --> Redis + Postgres (users/games)
                                                        +--> [logger/write] --> NFS .txt files --> [logger/upload] --> S3/B2
[streams-process] --> Kafka(streams-id) --> [streams-id] --helix /streams?user_id--> Kafka(streams)  (loop back)
[streams-export] reads Postgres directly --> NFS JSON files (no Kafka in practice)
```

---

## 1. Bot packages

### bot/streams (the poller) — `bot/streams/src/index.ts`
The primary Twitch fetcher. Runs as a **Kubernetes CronJob every 10 minutes** (`deploy/streams/streams.yaml:7` → `schedule: '*/10 * * * *'`), runs once and exits.

- Calls Helix `GET /helix/streams` paginated, `first: 100` per page (`index.ts:71,88`), following `pagination.cursor` in a `do/while` loop (line 78-149).
- For each page it wraps `result.data` into a `StreamsMessage` and publishes to the `streams` topic via `producer.sendBatch` (line 100-117). Message key `'stream'`, timestamp = now.
- **Stopping condition**: paginates downward by viewer count; when the last stream on a page has `viewer_count < config.minViewers` (default **5**, `index.ts:33`) it sends a final message with `endConfig {updateStartTime, update:true}` and breaks (line 119-148). So each run polls *all streams down to ~5 viewers*.
- No DB writes here — pure Twitch→Kafka producer.

### bot/streams-process (the DB writer) — `bot/streams-process/src/index.ts` + `processing.ts`
Long-running **Deployment** (`deploy/streams-process/streams-process.yaml`). Kafka consumer group `stream-process`, subscribes to `streams` topic (`index.ts:66-68`). This is where nearly all stream data lands in Postgres.

Per message (`processing.ts:87` `processStreams`), it runs a sequence of **batched multi-row INSERT ... ON CONFLICT** statements (all via `buildMultiInsert`, one SQL statement per table per batch):
- `probe` — viewer-count time series: `(stream_id,user_id,viewers,time)` ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING (`processing.ts:254-266`)
- `user_online` — currently-live tracking: `(user_id,stream_id,last_update)` ON CONFLICT DO UPDATE last_update (`239-252`)
- `stream` — one row per stream, ON CONFLICT DO UPDATE title/tags/game_id/updated_at, clears `ended_at` (`268-289`)
- Before updating, `splitNewAndOld` (`339-375`) does a `SELECT * FROM stream WHERE stream_id IN (...)` to diff, then writes change-history tables:
  - `stream_game` `(stream_id,game_id,time)` (`291-302`)
  - `stream_title` `(stream_id,title,time)` (`304-315`)
  - `stream_tags` `(stream_id,tag,time)` (`317-337`)
- `game_id` sanitized to `'0'` if non-numeric (`assureGameId`, line 80-85).

`processEnd` (`processing.ts:121`): on the `endConfig` message it finds `user_online` rows with `last_update < updateStartTime` (streams that dropped off), sets their `stream.ended_at`/`updated_at` = last_update + 5 min, deletes from `user_online`, and emits their `user_id`s to the `streams-id` topic for a confirmation re-poll. Note: these end-updates are done as **per-row sequential `pool.query` in a for-loop** (line 136-151) — not batched.

### bot/streams-id (targeted re-poll) — `bot/streams-id/src/index.ts`
Deployment, consumer group `streams-id`, consumes `streamsIdTopic`. For each `StreamsByIdMessage.ids` it calls Helix `GET /helix/streams?user_id=...` in chunks of 100 (`index.ts:65-99`) and republishes results back to the `streams` topic — closing the loop so streams-process can confirm end-of-stream.

### bot/missing (user/game enrichment) — `bot/missing/src/index.ts`, `missing.ts`, `prefix.ts`
Deployment, consumer group `stream-missing`, consumes `streams` topic. For every batch it extracts user_ids + game_ids and checks **Redis** (`mGet` with prefixes `user_id_`, `game_id_`, `prefix.ts`) to see which are unknown (`missing.ts:69-84`). Unknown ones are fetched from Helix `GET /users?id=` (chunks of 100) and `GET /games?id=`, written to Postgres via the database library (`insertUpdateStreamers`, `insertViewsProbes`, `insertUpdateGames`), then their IDs cached in Redis via `mSet` in chunks of 1000 (`insertIds`, line 57-67). Redis is the dedup filter that keeps it from re-fetching the ~same streamers every 10 min.
- `initRedis` (`missing.ts:99`) warms the cache from Postgres on startup (streamers/games with `created_at/updated_at > last cached time`).
- Two standalone maintenance scripts: `fullUpdateMissing.ts` (streams every `stream` row, re-fetches all missing users/games; batch=100) and `fullUpdateRedis.ts` (rebuilds the whole Redis cache from `game`/`streamers` tables, `--batchSize` default **1000**, line 23).

### bot/logger (Kafka→file archival) — `bot/logger/src/`
- `write.ts` — Deployment, consumer group `stream-log`, consumes `streams` topic, and appends each raw message to daily rolling files via `FileWriter` on an **NFS mount** (`deploy/logger/logger.yaml`: nfs server `10.30.2.1:/srv/storage` at `/log`).
- `FileWriter.ts` — appends `{"time":...,"data":...}\n` lines to `YYYY-MM-DD.txt` (`FileWriter.ts:52`) using a single append `fs.createWriteStream({flags:'a'})`; rolls file when the day-of-month changes. This is a streaming append (reasonably efficient) — one open stream, buffered writes.
- `read.ts` — replay tool: reads a `.txt` file line-by-line and re-publishes into Kafka (forces `endConfig.update=false`).
- `upload.ts` — **CronJob daily at 00:15** (`deploy/logger/upload.yaml:7`). Uploads `.txt` files older than `deleteAfter` days (default 5) to S3/Backblaze (`aws-sdk`), then `rm`s them.

### bot/streams-export (Postgres→JSON files) — `bot/streams-export/src/index.ts`
**This is the file-system storage attempt and the likely slow path.** Standalone job (no deployment manifest found). Purpose: archive ended streams out of Postgres into JSON files.
- Streams `SELECT * FROM stream WHERE ended_at is not null AND ended_at < now()-age` (default age = **7 days**, `index.ts:39`) via `pg-query-stream` (line 180-195).
- **Per-row processing pauses the stream and does synchronous serial work** (`processStreamRow`, line 110-174):
  - `fs.promises.mkdir(recursive)` a nested directory layout: `{path}/{user_id}/{YYYY}/{MM}/` (line 113-120)
  - Runs **4 separate per-stream SELECT queries** (`stream_game`, `stream_tags`, `probe`, `stream_title`, line 123-141) — one round-trip each, no joins/batching
  - `fs.promises.writeFile` **one JSON file per stream**, pretty-printed with indentation, named `{started_at ISO}-{stream_id}.json` (line 149-154)
  - Optionally 5 `DELETE` queries per stream if `--delete` (line 156-172)
- **Why it's slow**: fully serialized (`stream.pause()`/`resume()` around each row, line 190-195), one file write + `mkdir` + 4-5 DB round-trips per stream, pretty-printed JSON, many small files scattered across a deep per-user/year/month directory tree. Lots of `console.log` per row too. No batching anywhere.

---

## 2. Prisma schema — `libraries/prisma/prisma/stats_schema.prisma`

`provider = "postgresql"`, `url = env("STATS_DATABASE_URL")`, client generated to `../generated/stats-client`. Note the schema is **introspected** (`prisma db pull`) — Postgres is the source of truth, Prisma is only used by streams-export as a type/client dep. IDs are `BigInt`, timestamps `Timestamptz(6)`.

Models / tables:
| Model | Primary key | Indexes | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| `game` | `game_id` | — | name, box_art_url, updated_at |
| `probe` | `(stream_id,user_id,time)` | — | viewer-count time series |

# Twitch-Stats Monorepo: Architecture & Data-Flow Report

## Overall architecture / how pieces communicate

The system is an **event-driven pipeline glued together by Apache Kafka** (kafkajs), with **PostgreSQL** as the system-of-record and **Redis** as a dedup cache. There is no direct service-to-service calling — everything flows through Kafka topics. Deployed on Kubernetes (kustomize manifests under `deploy/`).

Kafka topics (defaults defined in `libraries/config/src/index.ts:69-73`):
- `twitch-stats-streams` — raw stream batches (`streamsTopic`)
- `twitch-stats-streams-id` — user IDs needing a targeted re-poll (`streamsIdTopic`)
- `twitch-stats-exported-stream` — export notifications (`exportTopic`, largely unused)

Pipeline:
```
[streams CronJob] --helix /streams--> Kafka(streams) --+--> [streams-process] --> Postgres
                                                        +--> [missing] --> Redis + Postgres (users/games)
                                                        +--> [logger/write] --> NFS .txt files --> [logger/upload] --> S3/B2
[streams-process] --> Kafka(streams-id) --> [streams-id] --helix /streams?user_id--> Kafka(streams)  (loop back)
[streams-export] reads Postgres directly --> NFS JSON files (no Kafka in practice)
```

---

## 1. Bot packages

### bot/streams (the poller) — `bot/streams/src/index.ts`
The primary Twitch fetcher. Runs as a **Kubernetes CronJob every 10 minutes** (`deploy/streams/streams.yaml:7` → `schedule: '*/10 * * * *'`), runs once and exits.

- Calls Helix `GET /helix/streams` paginated, `first: 100` per page (`index.ts:71,88`), following `pagination.cursor` in a `do/while` loop (line 78-149).
- For each page it wraps `result.data` into a `StreamsMessage` and publishes to the `streams` topic via `producer.sendBatch` (line 100-117). Message key `'stream'`, timestamp = now.
- **Stopping condition**: paginates downward by viewer count; when the last stream on a page has `viewer_count < config.minViewers` (default **5**, `index.ts:33`) it sends a final message with `endConfig {updateStartTime, update:true}` and breaks (line 119-148). - No DB writes here — pure Twitch→Kafka producer.

Long-running **Deployment** (`deploy/streams-process/streams-process.yaml`). Kafka consumer group `stream-process`, subscribes to `streams` topic (`index.ts:66-68`). This is where nearly all stream data lands in Postgres.

- `user_online` — currently-live tracking: `(user_id,stream_id,last_update)` ON CONFLICT DO UPDATE last_update (`239-252`)

  - `stream_game` `(stream_id,game_id,time)` (`291-302`)
  - `stream_title` `(stream_id,title,time)` (`304-315`)
  - `stream_tags` `(stream_id,tag,time)` (`317-337`)
- `game_id` sanitized to `'0'` if non-numeric (`assureGameId`, line 80-85).

Deployment, consumer group `streams-id`, consumes `streamsIdTopic`. Deployment, consumer group `stream-missing`, consumes `streams` topic. - `read.ts` — replay tool: reads a `.txt` file line-by-line and re-publishes into Kafka (forces `endConfig.update=false`).
- `upload.ts` — **CronJob daily at 00:15** (`deploy/logger/upload.yaml:7`). Uploads `.txt` files older than `deleteAfter` days (default 5) to S3/Backblaze (`aws-sdk`), then `rm`s them.

**This is the file-system storage attempt and the likely slow path.** Standalone job (no deployment manifest found). Purpose: archive ended streams out of Postgres into JSON files.
- Streams `SELECT * FROM stream WHERE ended_at is not null AND ended_at < now()-age` (default age = **7 days**, `index.ts:39`) via `pg-query-stream` (line 180-195).

content.text:
The analysis is done. Here's what I found and what I'd build.

## Why your two attempts got slow

Your pipeline is: `streams` CronJob (every 10 min, all streams ≥5 viewers ≈ tens of thousands) → Kafka → `streams-process` → Postgres, with `missing` enriching users/games and `logger` archiving raw Kafka to daily files → B2.

**Attempt 1 (Postgres):** The killer is `probe` — one row per live stream every 10 minutes, so tens of thousands of rows per run, billions over months. All history lives in one unpartitioned table with a composite PK, so every insert has to update ever-growing B-tree indexes that no longer fit in RAM → random I/O → inserts crawl. `stream_title`/`stream_game` have the same shape, just smaller. Also `processEnd` in `bot/streams-process/src/processing.ts:136-151` updates ended streams one `pool.query` per row.

**Attempt 2 (filesystem, `bot/streams-export`):** One pretty-printed JSON file per stream in a `{user_id}/{year}/{month}/` tree, and per stream it does a `mkdir` + 4–5 sequential SELECTs + one `writeFile`, fully serialized via `stream.pause()`/`resume()` — all on NFS, where small-file creates cost a full round trip each. Millions of streams × ~6 serial I/O ops each is why it can't keep up. Notably, your `logger` FileWriter (single append stream, daily rolling files) has no such problem — that's the pattern to reuse.

## Recommended design: hot/cold split with per-stream archive documents

Postgres only ever holds the **live + recent window** (~14 days). All history lives on B2 as compressed per-stream JSON documents, appended in batches — never one file per stream.

**1. Hot store (Postgres, bounded size forever)**
- Partition `probe`, `stream_title`, `stream_game`, `stream_tags` by day (native range partitioning). Inserts always hit a small today-partition whose index fits in RAM — insert speed stays constant regardless of history size.
- Retention: `DROP PARTITION` for anything older than 14 days. No DELETEs, no vacuum churn.
- Batch the `processEnd` loop into one multi-row statement while we're at it.

**2. Archival on stream end (replaces `streams-export`)**
- `streams-process` already detects ended streams; have it emit them to a `stream-ended` Kafka topic.
- A new archiver consumer takes ended streams in batches of a few hundred, fetches their probes/titles/games with **4 IN-list queries per batch** (not 4 per stream), and builds one document per stream:

```json
{
  "stream_id": "...", "user_id": "...", "login": "...",
  "stream": { ...last raw helix stream object... },
  "started_at": "...", "ended_at": "...",
  "viewers": [[time, count], ...],
  "titles": [{"time": ..., "title": ...}],
  "games": [{"time": ..., "game_id": ...}],
  "tags":  [{"time": ..., "tags": [...]}]
}
```

- Each document is individually zstd/gzip-compressed and **appended to one rolling per-day archive file** (same FileWriter pattern as `logger`), then uploaded to B2 daily by the existing upload CronJob. Because each doc is its own compression frame, you can later fetch a single stream with an S3/B2 **range GET** using offset+length — no need to download the whole day file.
- Since everything older than 14 days is already archived, partition drops are safe. Edge case: 24/7 channels / subathons running longer than the window — checkpoint them with partial documents (`part: n`) so no probes are lost.

**3. Slim index + summaries stay in Postgres (this is what your site queries)**
- `archive_stream(stream_id, user_id, started_at, ended_at, file, offset, length)` — one tiny row per stream, points into the archive.
- `stream_summary(stream_id, user_id, duration, avg_viewers, peak_viewers, games[], ...)` — precomputed at archive time. This is what powers TwitchTracker/SullyGnome-style streamer pages and rankings; one row per stream (~a few hundred K/day) is trivial for Postgres.

**Why this holds up:** the write path is append-only to one open file (your logger already proves this keeps up with the full firehose), DB round-trips are ~4 per few hundred streams instead of ~5 per stream, and cold storage is ~300–500 MB/day compressed on B2 — around $0.05–0.08/month per month of history. Postgres stays at a fixed ~14-day working set, so insert performance never degrades again.

**Alternative considered:** self-hosted ClickHouse would swallow this insert volume raw and compress ~15–20×, and is the better call if you want ad-hoc analytics over all history ("avg viewers by game across 2 years"). But it's a new stateful component to operate; the archive-document design reuses your existing Kafka/NFS/B2 machinery and matches your stated access pattern (per-stream JSON + per-streamer stats). Easy to add ClickHouse later by replaying archive files into it.

Assumption I made: your query needs are per-streamer/per-stream pages plus leaderboard-style aggregates, not arbitrary ad-hoc queries over raw probes. If that's wrong, ClickHouse moves to the front.

Want me to implement it? Natural order: (1) day-partitioning + retention for the probe/history tables, (2) the `stream-ended` topic + archiver service, (3) the summary/index tables.

content: Goal: fast, cheap storage for Twitch stream history. I analyzed the codebase and proposed a hot/cold design: Postgres keeps ~14 days, ended streams archive as compressed JSON docs to B2. Next: your go-ahead to implement, starting with table partitioning. (disable recaps in /config)

message.content: why json per day and not per stream?

content.text:
Because file *creation* is the expensive part, not the bytes — especially on NFS and object storage.

**Write path.** One file per stream means per stream: `mkdir` (possibly 3 levels), open, write, close — each a metadata round trip to the NFS server, a few ms each, fully serialized. At ~300k ended streams/day that's over a million metadata ops, which is exactly why your `streams-export` couldn't keep up. Appending to one already-open file is just buffered sequential writes — your `logger` proves that keeps up with the entire raw firehose in real time.

**Object storage.** B2/S3 price and throttle per-request. 300k tiny PUTs/day vs ~1 upload/day, and small objects compress poorly on their own (a 1–2 KB JSON gzips much worse than the same doc inside a shared dictionary window; zstd frames in a big file also share OS-level write batching). Also listing/managing millions of small objects gets slow and costs money on every lifecycle operation.

**Filesystem health.** Millions of small files = inode exhaustion, slow `ls`/backup/rsync, and deep directory trees that themselves become a bottleneck.

**And you lose nothing on reads.** Because each stream's doc is its own compression frame at a known `(file, offset, length)` — stored in the `archive_stream` index row — fetching one stream is a single range GET that returns exactly that stream's bytes. Functionally identical to per-stream files, one HTTP request either way.

Per-stream files only win if you want to browse the archive by hand without the index. If that matters, a fallback is possible (scan a day file sequentially), but I wouldn't design for it.

message.content: i would not even write to the filesystem anymore, just write to S3

content.text:
Agreed — that's cleaner. The filesystem step was only inherited from your logger pattern; it isn't needed. B2/S3 can't append, but you don't need append: buffer completed stream docs in memory and flush as **rolling chunk objects**.

Adjusted archiver design:

- Keep an in-memory buffer of compressed stream docs. Flush when it hits ~32–64 MB **or** every ~15 min, whichever comes first, as one object: `archive/2026/07/17/1430-<uuid>.jsonl.zst`.
- Each doc is still its own zstd frame at a recorded offset, so `archive_stream(object_key, offset, length)` + range GET still fetches a single stream in one request.
- Ordering per flush: PUT object → insert index + summary rows in one PG transaction → commit Kafka offsets. Crash at any point just replays the batch; index upserts (`ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING`) make it idempotent. The re-uploaded orphan chunk from a partial flush is harmless garbage (add a lifecycle rule or ignore it — index never points there).

What this buys you: no NFS mount, no upload CronJob, no local disk state at all — the archiver becomes a stateless deployment where Kafka is the write-ahead log and the only durability requirement is "Kafka retention > flush interval," which you have by orders of magnitude.

Two knock-on simplifications worth taking:
- **Memory risk is trivial** — ~500 MB/day means a 15-min buffer is ~5 MB. You could even flush hourly and stay tiny.
- **`logger` can adopt the same pattern** — the raw Kafka archival currently doing NFS + daily upload could also buffer-and-PUT directly, retiring the NFS volume entirely. Slightly bigger buffers (raw feed is larger), same mechanics.

One caveat: lots of small PUTs would cost money on B2's class-A calls, but at ~50–100 objects/day this is fractions of a cent. Just don't flush per-stream — that would recreate the small-object problem we just designed away.

message.content: where do you keep the buffer?

content.text:
Just in the archiver process's RAM — an array of compressed `Buffer`s plus a running byte offset. Nothing on disk anywhere.

That's safe because of the commit ordering: Kafka offsets are only committed **after** the flush (PUT + index transaction) succeeds. The buffer is always a replayable suffix of the Kafka topic, so:

- **Process crashes mid-buffer** → offsets were never committed → on restart the consumer re-reads those same `stream-ended` messages and rebuilds the exact same docs. Kafka *is* the durable buffer; RAM is just staging.
- **Crash mid-flush** (object uploaded, transaction not committed) → same replay; a new chunk object gets written and indexed, the orphan from the failed attempt is never referenced.

Practical details that make this work with kafkajs:

- Use manual offset management (`autoCommit: false`, `commitOffsets()` after flush), tracking the highest offset per partition currently sitting in the buffer.
- On a **consumer-group rebalance** (deploy, scale, pod eviction), discard the buffer without committing — the partition's new owner replays those messages. Cheap, since the buffer is minutes of data.
- Size it by count/bytes, not trust: cap at, say, 64 MB and force a flush — but at your volume a 15-min buffer is single-digit MB, so the cap is a safety rail, not a tuning knob.

The one real requirement this imposes: Kafka retention on `stream-ended` must comfortably exceed your worst-case archiver downtime (retention of a few days vs. a flush interval of minutes — huge margin).

message.content: ok, show me your plan again

content.text:
## Plan: hot/cold storage for stream history

### Architecture

```
streams (cron, 10min) ──► Kafka(streams) ──┬─► streams-process ──► Postgres (hot, ~14 days)
                                           │        │
                                           │        └─► Kafka(stream-ended)
                                           │                 │
                                           └─► missing       ▼
                                                        archiver ──► B2 chunk objects
                                                            └─────► Postgres index + summaries
```

Postgres holds only live + recent streams; full history lives on B2 as compressed per-stream JSON documents packed into rolling chunk objects. No NFS, no per-stream files.

### 1. Bound the hot store (Postgres)

- Range-partition `probe`, `stream_title`, `stream_game`, `stream_tags` by day. Inserts always hit a small today-partition → insert speed stays constant forever.
- Retention job: `DROP PARTITION` older than 14 days (safe because anything that old is already archived). No DELETEs, no vacuum churn.
- Fix `processEnd` (`bot/streams-process/src/processing.ts:136-151`): batch the per-row loop into multi-row statements.

### 2. Emit stream endings

- `streams-process` already detects ended streams in `processEnd` — additionally publish them to a new Kafka topic `stream-ended`.

### 3. Archiver (new bot, replaces `streams-export`)

- Consumes `stream-ended` in batches of a few hundred; fetches probes/titles/games/tags with **4 IN-list queries per batch**.
- Builds one JSON doc per stream: last raw helix stream object, `viewers: [[time, count]...]`, title/game/tag change lists.
- Each doc individually zstd-compressed, appended to an **in-memory buffer** (array of Buffers + running offset).
- Flush at ~15 min or 64 MB, whichever first, as one object: `archive/YYYY/MM/DD/HHmm-<uuid>.jsonl.zst`.
- Ordering per flush: PUT to B2 → one PG transaction inserting index + summary rows (`ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING`) → commit Kafka offsets (`autoCommit: false`). Crash or rebalance at any point = drop buffer, replay from Kafka. Stateless pod; Kafka is the WAL.
- Checkpoint streams live longer than the retention window (subathons) as partial docs (`part: n`) so no probes are lost to partition drops.

### 4. Query layer (Postgres, small forever)

- `archive_stream(stream_id, user_id, started_at, ended_at, object_key, offset, length)` — fetch any single stream's full doc with one B2 **range GET**.
- `stream_summary(stream_id, user_id, duration, avg_viewers, peak_viewers, games[], ...)` — computed at archive time; powers streamer pages and rankings without touching the archive.

### 5. Cleanup (optional, later)

- Port `logger` to the same buffer→PUT pattern, retiring the NFS volume and the upload CronJob entirely.
- Delete `streams-export`.

**Cost/perf envelope:** ~300–500 MB/day compressed on B2 (~$0.05–0.08/month per month of history), ~50–100 PUTs/day, Postgres fixed at a ~14-day working set.

**Build order:** (1) partitioning + retention, (2) `stream-ended` topic + batched `processEnd`, (3) archiver, (4) summary/index tables + wire into site, (5) logger cleanup.

Say go and I'll start with step 1.

message.content: instead of B2 i want to user hetzner object storage

content.text:
Fine — zero design impact. Hetzner Object Storage is S3-compatible, so the archiver still talks the same S3 API (`PutObject`, range `GetObject`); only endpoint and credentials change:

- Endpoint: `https://<region>.your-objectstorage.com` (fsn1/nbg1/hel1 — pick the region your hcloud cluster runs in, keeps GETs low-latency and traffic internal to Hetzner's network).
- Works with the `aws-sdk` you already use in `logger` — set `endpoint`, `s3ForcePathStyle: true` (or virtual-hosted style, both work), dummy region.
- Credentials via a SOPS-encrypted secret in your flux repo, per your existing convention.

It's actually a better fit than B2 for this design:

- **Flat pricing** (base fee includes 1 TB storage + 1 TB traffic) with no per-request charges — the PUT-frequency caveat from the B2 plan disappears entirely. At ~15 GB/month growth you're inside the base tier for years.
- **Same network as the cluster** — range GETs for stream pages are a few ms instead of a cross-provider hop.

One thing to be aware of: Hetzner enforces per-bucket rate limits (on the order of hundreds of requests/s). Irrelevant for the archiver's ~100 PUTs/day, but if the site later serves stream pages straight from range GETs at high traffic, put a small cache in front rather than hammering the bucket.

Plan stays as before, with B2 → Hetzner and the lifecycle/orphan-cleanup done via S3 lifecycle rules (Hetzner supports those too). Ready to start on step 1 (partitioning + retention) whenever you say go.
